question.text: In which scenario does a short hedge using bond futures perform BETTER than selling the bonds and investing in T-bills?

answer.text: When interest rates rise significantly — the futures gain compensates for the bond price decline while retaining accrued interest on the held bonds
When interest rates fall — the bond price appreciation adds to the futures gain
When interest rates remain stable — the futures converge to spot price at zero gain/loss
In all scenarios — futures hedging always outperforms selling bonds

explanation.text: **The Logic:** From Series IV Ch5 scenario comparison: Holding bonds + short futures = Rs. 50,881,527. Selling bonds + T-bills = Rs. 50,753,106. The futures hedge outperformed because: (1) accrued interest was earned on held bonds, and (2) futures gain offset the price decline. Selling bonds meant losing accrued interest and earning lower T-bill returns. **The Trap:** Futures hedging doesn't always outperform — if rates fall, the futures loss reduces gains. The advantage is mainly in rising rate scenarios where bonds would be sold at a loss. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch5 provides detailed scenario comparisons for bond hedging alternatives. Series XIII compares hedging approaches across asset classes. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Futures hedging preserves the income stream (coupons/accrued interest) from holding the bonds while protecting against price risk — selling bonds sacrifices this income.
